data.title: Robinhood restricts prediction products; Trump says he will begin blocking the Strait of Hormuz.
data.description:
Headline

▌Robinhood has proactively restricted some prediction market products due to concerns about market manipulation and insider trading risks

Robinhood stated that due to concerns about market manipulation and insider trading risks, it has proactively excluded some prediction market products. It is understood that the company pays close attention to market manipulation issues when developing this business and will not offer users all types of event contracts. Recent "accurate betting" incidents have drawn regulatory attention. Furthermore, "market mentions" (such as betting on words that will appear in speeches) are also considered easily manipulated, becoming one of the categories that Robinhood explicitly does not offer. Currently, Robinhood primarily provides compliant prediction market services through partnerships with Kalshi and ForecastEx. It is understood that it will prioritize regulated platforms and products to reduce information misuse and cross-border compliance risks.

▌Trump: US Navy to Begin Blockade of Strait of Hormuz

US President Trump: The talks (between the US and Iran) went well, and most issues were agreed upon, but the only truly important issue, the nuclear one, was not settled. Effective immediately, the US Navy will begin a blockade of any ships attempting to enter or leave the Strait of Hormuz. I have also instructed the US Navy to search and seize every ship in international waters that has paid passage fees to Iran.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $70,644.41, a 24-hour change of -3.5%; ETH price is $2,189.35, a 24-hour change of -4.4%; BNB price is $591.36, a 24-hour change of -2.7%. The price of SOL is $81.45, a 24-hour change of -4.3%; the price of DOGE is $0.09066, a 24-hour change of -2.7%; the price of XRP is $1.32, a 24-hour change of -2.4%; and the price of TRX is $0.3216, a 24-hour change of +0.8%. The price of WLFI is $0.07832, with a 24-hour change of -2.1%; the price of HYPE is $40.82, with a 24-hour change of -3.6%. **Policy** ▌Argentine Securities Regulatory Agency Includes Crypto Assets in Qualified Investor Criteria On April 13, the Argentine National Securities Commission (CNV) recently issued Resolution No. 1125/2026, revising the definition of qualified investors and explicitly including virtual assets in the scope of assets that can be used to determine investor qualifications. According to the new regulations, virtual assets held by individuals or legal entities can be combined with securities investments and bank deposits for calculation. A minimum of 350,000 UVA (Argentine inflation-linked units) qualifies an investor as a qualified investor. Furthermore, the revisions include new provisions related to crowdfunding (Financiamiento Colectivo), allowing non-qualified investors to participate in specific public offerings. The maximum investment per transaction is 3,000 UVA, with a cumulative maximum of 10,000 UVA, and cannot exceed 5% (per transaction) and 10% (cumulative) of the investor's personal assets.

▌Iran Responds to Trump: Blocking Iran Will Not "Open" the Strait of Hormuz

April 12th - US President Trump retweeted an article on social media about a "naval blockade" of Iran. The Iranian Embassy in Austria responded that same day, stating that a blockade cannot "open" the Strait of Hormuz.

The embassy wrote on social media: "A simple explanation to those concerned: a blockade is a blockade. It cannot 'open' the Strait of Hormuz, it can only restrict it. The only way out of this self-inflicted predicament is to remain open and flexible in negotiations. Follow reason." (Xinhua) (CCTV International News)

Blockchain Applications

▌Tether-affiliated Super PAC's first $300,000 expenditure raises questions about potential insider dealings

On April 13, according to CoinDesk, documents filed with the Federal Election Commission (FEC) by the Tether-affiliated Super PAC Fellowship show that its first $300,000 expenditure went to Nxum Group, a company co-founded by Tether US CEO and former Trump administration crypto advisor Bo Hines, along with his father and third-party partners. The expenditure was used to purchase campaign ads for Georgia Republican congressional candidate Clay Fuller. On April 1, the Fellowship appointed Tether US Vice President of Regulatory Affairs Jesse Spiro as chairman of the committee.

The committee received a total of $100 million in funding commitments when it was announced last year, but FEC filings show that the account balance is currently zero. Tether International responded that it has no connection with the Fellowship, while Tether U.S. declined to comment. JPMorgan Chase will expand JPM Coin to Canton Network through Kinexys in 2026. On April 13, JPMorgan Chase announced that it will expand JPM Coin to Canton Network through Kinexys in 2026. This network currently settles more than $350 billion daily in U.S. Treasury repurchase transactions. Cryptocurrency

Tok-Edge to Launch Crypto Hedge Fund and Raise $100 Million

Digital asset financial services company Tok-Edge announced the launch of a crypto hedge fund for institutional investors and plans to raise $100 million in its first round of funding in 2026. Tok-Edge, which had previously been operating discreetly, reportedly aims to build a compliant crypto asset allocation infrastructure for institutions. The fund's initial size is $21 million, with veteran traditional finance investor Marcus Meijer and his investment consortium as investors, the latter planning to provide $10 million as a cornerstone investor.

Cryptocurrency

▌Tok-Edge to Launch Crypto Hedge Fund and Raise $100 Million

▌European banks are actively advancing their respective stablecoin plans

On April 13, Paris-based Société Générale positioned its stablecoin in areas such as cross-border payments, on-chain settlement, foreign exchange, and cash management, while another Paris-based bank, Oddo BHF, launched a euro stablecoin compliant with the MiCA standard. A consortium of banks, including ING, UniCredit, and BNP Paribas, is preparing a Swiss franc stablecoin, planned for launch in the second half of 2026.

...

▌ClearBank Receives MiCA Approval, Plans to Launch Stablecoin and Deepen Partnership with Coinbase

On April 13, Netherlands-based ClearBank received approval from the Dutch Financial Markets Authority (AFM) to operate as a Crypto Asset Service Provider (CASP) under the Crypto Asset Markets Regulation Act (MiCA). ClearBank plans to offer EURC and USDC stablecoin services to support more efficient cross-border transfers and payments. Furthermore, ClearBank will deepen its partnership with Coinbase, including offering FSCS-covered savings accounts through Coinbase. ClearBank Europe is currently authorized by the European Central Bank and regulated by the Dutch Central Bank (DNB).

▌Michael Saylor Hints at Imminent Bitcoin Purchases

On April 13, “Looking to the future,” Michael Saylor said on Sunday while sharing a chart of Strategy’s Bitcoin purchase history, which has become a sign of impending Bitcoin accumulation. According to the company, Strategy’s most recent Bitcoin purchase was on April 6, when it bought 4,871 Bitcoins, worth over $329.8 million, bringing its total holdings to 766,970 Bitcoins, worth approximately $54.5 billion at the market price at the time of publication.

...

▌Due to geopolitical conflicts and energy issues, several mining farms are selling Bitcoin

On April 13, data showed that MARA Holdings sold more than 15,000 Bitcoins in March to reduce leverage, Riot Platforms sold more than 3,700 Bitcoins in the first quarter, Cango sold 2,000 Bitcoins to repay Bitcoin-backed debt, and Bitdeer stated that as of February 20, its Bitcoin holdings had dropped to zero.

▌Justin Sun accuses WLFI of having a hidden blacklist backdoor in its token contract

On April 13, Justin Sun accused WLFI of embedding a secret blacklist function in its WLFI smart contract, giving it the power to unilaterally freeze token holders' wallets without prior notice or any remedy.

He stated that his wallet was frozen in 2025. Justin Sun wrote on X: "This is a trapdoor disguised as an open door." Important Economic Developments International crude oil prices surged 9.4% on Monday, with WTI crude rising 9.4% to $104.54 per barrel, following the failure of the weekend talks between the US and Iran and Trump's blockade of Iranian maritime traffic. All members of the US negotiating team have left Pakistan. A US official stated on April 12 that all members of the US negotiating team had left Pakistan after the US-Iran talks concluded. The official stated that personnel including Witkov, Kushner, and the technical team did not remain in the Pakistani capital, indicating that the two sides would not immediately resume direct negotiations. Earlier that day, Vance said at a press conference in Islamabad, the Pakistani capital, that the US had not yet reached a consensus with Iran and would return to the United States. The US-Iran negotiations in Islamabad ended on the 12th without any agreement. The Iranian side said the negotiations were conducted in an "atmosphere of distrust and suspicion," with disagreements on two or three key issues. The US side stated that it had clearly stated its "red lines," but Iran did not accept the US conditions. (CCTV News) (Xinhua News Agency) Trump Threatens to Strike Iranian Economy by Controlling Strait of Hormuz On April 12, US President Trump retweeted an article on his social media, stating that if Iran refuses to compromise, its trump card might be controlling the Strait of Hormuz through the US Navy to prohibit Iranian oil tankers from entering or leaving, thereby striking at the Iranian economy. (CCTV News) According to CME's "FedWatch" on April 10, the probability of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 1.6%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 98.4%. The probability of the Federal Reserve cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 2.1%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 96.3%, and the probability of cumulatively raising interest rates by 25 basis points is 1.5%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Stocks surge after "ceasefire," but Wall Street warns: damage has already been done.
data.description:
The news of a ceasefire in the Middle East briefly boosted risk assets, but Wall Street strategists warned that the war has left deep wounds on inflation, energy supplies, and the Federal Reserve's policy space that are difficult to heal quickly. This week, benchmark U.S. stock indexes rose across the board, with the S&P 500 surging 3.6%, its biggest weekly gain since the end of November last year. However, the S&P's rally weakened in midday trading on Friday, closing lower as markets worried whether the peace talks this weekend could end the six-week-long war that has deeply impacted the economy.

Institutions maintain target prices while simultaneously lowering them

Currently, most institutions have chosen to maintain their full-year target prices for the time being, but their reasons and confidence vary.

Luca Paolini, chief strategist at Pictet Asset Management, said that influenced by Tuesday's ceasefire news, the team, which was close to adjusting its positions, has postponed its actions. They now expect the S&P 500 to close at 7250 points by the end of the year, European stocks to rise by about 10%, and the 10-year US Treasury yield to fall below 4.25%.

Pictet expects the Federal Reserve and the Bank of England to each cut interest rates once, while the European Central Bank will remain on hold.

Scott Chronert, head of US equity strategy at Citigroup, maintained his optimistic forecast released in mid-December, arguing that "most of the current situation appears to be transitional."

However, he also acknowledged that risks cannot be ignored, including persistently high oil prices leading to prolonged high interest rates, pressure on the private credit market, and potential disruptions from AI and Trump's tariff policies. He pointed out that upward revisions to earnings expectations are concentrated in a few large-cap stocks such as Nvidia and Broadcom, indicating that market rotation has been significantly hampered. He said: "This is a market still searching for direction, and it is too early to make definitive judgments." Wells Fargo was one of the few institutions to lower its full-year forecast, reducing its S&P 500 target price from 7800 to 7300. Chief equity strategist Ohsung Kwon believes the current economy is less sensitive to oil prices than in past cycles, and tax refunds will partially offset pressure on household consumption. However, Kwon says: "Unless we see a significant deterioration in earnings-driven growth, we still expect the stock market to perform solidly throughout the year."

data.description:

data.title: BlackRock's Global Chief Fixed Income Investment Officer Shares Investment Principles
data.description:
Source: Hard Lessons podcast; Compiled by: Felix, PANews

Rick Rieder, Chief Investment Officer of Global Fixed Income at BlackRock, recently appeared on Morgan Stanley's Hard Lessons podcast to share his experience on investing, liquidity, and discipline. At BlackRock, Rick Rieder helps manage up to $2.7 trillion in assets across global bond and multi-asset markets and serves as Chairman of BlackRock's Wide Investment Council.

In the conversation, Rick Rieder explained the drivers of long-term returns and why seeking perspectives different from market consensus is crucial. PANews has compiled the highlights of the conversation.

Host: You've worked at BlackRock for 17 years, which is a long time. How has your journey at BlackRock been?

Rieder: I remember back in 2009, when I was running a hedge fund, all my partners were discussing whether we should join BlackRock. I remember saying that this could become the center of finance, but I never expected the company to grow to the size it is today. We joined just before the acquisitions of BGI and iShares, which significantly altered the landscape of the ETF industry. Think about it, now we manage over $14 trillion in assets, and when I joined, we were just a tiny fraction of that. It has become an amazing place, so it's been a very interesting experience. Host: You just used the word "center," and I think that's very apt, because when you were managing only a few billion dollars, did you ever imagine that one day you would be talking about managing trillions of dollars? Rieder: I'm the chairman of the board of a charter school in Newark, New Jersey. Once, I gave a presentation about the size of the funds we manage. I asked, "Does anyone know how many zeros are after a trillion?" Someone answered, but I didn't actually know myself. I had to stop and actually count the zeros in my head. It's kind of funny, but when you think about that massive scale, it's truly incredible. Of course, in the portfolios I manage, we have an almost insane level of precision in every detail. Therefore, managing at this scale doesn't feel out of control; it feels very manageable. Host: Do you think pure scale advantage is one of the secrets to your success? What exactly is the secret to success? Rieder: Scale does bring some benefits, such as being able to see huge flows of funds across different asset classes and thousands of different scenarios. Statistically speaking, being able to see so many different things naturally increases your chances of winning. By the way, it's not that all my decisions were right, or that many were right; the point is that you have the ability to observe and judge, "Okay, that makes sense," and to see relative value in places you wouldn't even think of. It's like being able to put all the pieces together. It gives you the ability to actually try and effectively put the puzzle together.

Host: So I'd like to shift the topic and try to draw some wisdom from you, and analyze a little bit about the process you went through when reflecting on your career. At times, your views contradicted the consensus, but you turned out to be right.

Rieder: Perhaps I need to clarify what you mean by "wisdom." I really don't think I should be described as having wisdom, because I feel it all depends on having to do a lot of work. At least for me, there are no unique skills that are innate. I just try to put all the pieces together and then hold firm to that belief.

Some people will confidently double down if others don't believe them, but I'm not so good at that. However, I think I'm better at observing things like technology. I'm a super tech geek. Whenever a new technology is released, I have to be first in line. My wife always laughs at me for having to run to the store, have to queue, have to be with everyone, and have to understand what it is. I just love technology. I'll never forget when electric cars first came out. Back then, many people said, "This won't work. Batteries are too expensive. You can't compete. There are so many established giants in the traditional gasoline car market. This is absolutely impossible." I remember doing all the research and finding that it wasn't really a car business, but an energy business. You have to consider whether it's efficient? Can you create real economies of scale around it? I remember sitting alone in a conference room, like the only fool. By the way, I don't mind expressing my strong convictions many times, but I'm telling you, getting involved early in these things is crucial. I remember my first test drive, and my feeling was, "Wow, it's fast, clean, and quiet. This is a better product." I have this feeling about many new things, like when the initial Mac computers and AirPods were released, I thought, "This works great, it's very different." But for me, the next step is to understand: How big is the total potential market (TAM)? What is the market size? How do you reduce costs over time? What will the back-end cash flow look like?

Host: You just described a very disciplined and humble process. But sometimes going it alone is also a lonely process. When everyone else says you might be crazy. What do you do in those moments?

Rieder: I would say that 99% of the time, it's thanks to teamwork, because everyone tends to conform to the consensus, and I'm no exception. When there are people who hold non-consensus views and are different from you, you often double-check with them, asking yourself, "What are we missing? Where did we go wrong?" Then I like to read a lot of analyses from people with different viewpoints to see why they think it makes no sense.

So I tend to talk to people who share my views, but for those who disagree, I try to research them and build my confidence. But as I've said before, over time I've learned that we're not in a business that "must be right," we're in a business that creates returns for our clients. The reality is that market perceptions can be wrong for much longer periods. I remember learning about the "efficient market hypothesis" in school, and I actually think they should throw that theory away because it's completely detached from reality. I believe the market often makes mistakes, but you have to survive because by the time the market theoretically becomes right, your capital might already be depleted. So I try to stay in the market. When people disagree with me, I'm not good at doubling down because that would damage my confidence, but I try to stick to my existing positions and try to analyze my thinking more deeply. It's like telling myself: this will definitely work. Host: I think at least Keynes said, "The market may remain irrational for longer than you can remain liquid." As a sell-side analyst, asking this might seem a bit self-serving, but do you find it more helpful to read articles by people who broadly agree with you to confirm your ideas, or to seek out those who disagree with you, stress-test them, and find where you might be going wrong? Rieder: The most mature answer would be: challenge yourself with people who hold different views, but I haven't done that enough. There are people I respect and trust, and I enjoy reading their analysis regardless of whether they're right or wrong. But when things are very contrary to consensus, I try to read a lot of the ideas of those who agree. Everyone tends to act in unison. I think social media has exacerbated this phenomenon. Everyone is going in the same direction, and as you get older and have been in this business longer, you'll find that there's a moment when everyone is acting, and prices move accordingly, and then you realize, "Okay, now that's the move, it's time to go against the trend." I think the market has largely become a casino mechanism now; you see everyone queuing up on one side, and at that point, as a contrarian investor going against the consensus, I think the profits become more substantial. Especially when news flow is very dense, this actually becomes a pretty good trading strategy: if the market leans to one side, you short it; if the market leans to the other side, you continue to trade against it. You'll realize you're on the trend's trajectory.

Host: But I also have to say, if the entire market is already on one side, then making money this way is even harder, because the price has already reflected everything. So what do you plan to do? Maybe sometimes you've carefully considered all the details, but ultimately the trade still didn't succeed; maybe the consensus was right, and there was a reason for it. When you recall those unsuccessful experiences, what comes to mind first?

Rieder: The trades I made that went wrong immediately come to mind, because I think you learn a lot more from making mistakes. People tend to forget, or hope that the results of a lifetime of training result in more correct than wrong answers. But I remember about 20 years ago, shortly after I graduated, in a bond coupon transaction, I thought I was definitely right, so I wanted to buy more.

That was an incredibly painful lesson: the whole world thinks you're wrong, you have to exit, or you have to reduce your position size so that one security doesn't ruin your entire career. That was my first lesson: you have to manage your risk, you have to manage your position size. It's a small industry, and everyone can sense you're in trouble. It was a very bad situation, and it's a pretty brutal industry, and when people think you're on the wrong side, they put pressure on you. I'll always remember Peloton (the fitness equipment company). I was one of its earliest investors, and it was incredible how I watched their technology develop, then the pandemic hit, which was lucky for them, and then the stock price exploded. Then you become even more immersed in its ecosystem. This is also one of those situations that makes you feel really bad: you think you're right about it, but suddenly it starts to go against you, and a lot of people are talking about why it's falling. I learned a few things in that process: how do you manage cash flow? The company grew too fast, and people say it's because of the COVID-19 pandemic, maybe that's partly true, but "stop-loss" is a very good habit. And I have a theory: if you're right three-quarters of the time, and then you go wrong, well, it's time to cut your losses and move on. Because in this industry, just one mistake can hurt you in an extremely drastic way. So, I firmly believe this, especially in bonds: diversify, make sure you have liquid assets. A 65% or 60% win rate is fine. A 70% win rate on illiquid assets is good too. I really think that as long as you're right more often than you're wrong, it's okay. Like running a casino. But in the stock market, the reason I remember those major mistakes so vividly is because in the stock market, you experience explosive profits and crashes, and that's exactly what you have to think about. You know, you have to cultivate the investments that make you money while managing the ones that make you lose. Make sure you keep your positions at a level where you can afford to lose. Host: Regarding the Peloton deal, do you think you were wrong in the first place, or was it simply a matter of timing? Rieder: Frankly, I think the company could have changed course, changed direction, but they didn't. I think they were too slow to change course. As someone with a credit background who later moved into equity investing, the most important thing I learned is this: in credit, you focus on cash flow, interest coverage ratio, collateral, and all those other metrics; with companies, you think about their business model and how they generate cash flow. But one thing I learned is that the people managing the company are extremely important, not just the individuals, but the team that runs the company. Because companies always evolve, industries evolve, technology evolves, and you have to know how to transform. Most of the most successful companies started out completely different from what they are now. Then they transformed, entered a field full of opportunities, and what they did initially might have been a good idea at the time, but later had to change. Now, I spend more time talking to CEOs to gauge whether they understand the data? Do they understand the business? Is someone a good operator? For me, that's the whole point.

Host: What allows you to sift through these details and make such a distinction?

Rieder: In the tech world, some people have a keen insight, able to think about where the world is going and then adapt their businesses accordingly. But there are also people, as you said, haphazard and disorganized, trying to do too much or chasing trends, and ending up lagging behind. Over time, I've found some very unique individuals who are exceptionally good at positioning their companies well before the wave hits. Some are on the crest of the wave, able to talk big, but the key is the person who can truly understand what's happening in the chaos and know how to adapt.

Host: I think this skill is actually universal in many different fields.

Rieder: That's true. But let me tell you one thing I've done very poorly throughout my career: reading.

There's so much analysis and research, so much brilliant stuff to read. If we were to divide the work and say, "You read this, you read that," I feel like I have to read as much as possible myself. I have meetings every month, and I think we have a great team. We brainstorm, and then I have to lock myself in a room and figure everything out myself. Hopefully, AI will be able to do this work for me in the future. But I guess I'll still use AI to help me organize my thoughts, just that it will allow me to absorb more information much faster. Host: Yes, my firm prediction is that AI will present all the information to you, but you'll still have to go through it yourself. Rieder: I think that's true. The decision-making process should become more efficient because I've found that as humans, you can only think on a limited number of levels. When I look at our portfolio and think about risk, if you can think multidimensionally and use technology for stress testing, scenario analysis, and combining different parts, I think that will be very valuable. Host: When you look back on your entire career, what lessons have been the most painful? Rieder: For me, the financial crisis is definitely one of the first, second, and third most painful lessons. Because I started a hedge fund before the financial crisis. You imagine financial assets, when all assets are falling highly correlated, and we were using some leverage, suddenly everything was falling in sync. Host: You started your hedge fund during the financial crisis? Rieder: Or rather, a few months before the crisis, when I actually thought it was a good time because things were becoming volatile, interesting, etc., but I didn't anticipate the series of events that followed. We started out doing well, everything was going smoothly, and then suddenly, the whole world was disrupted in a way that I found incredibly unexpected (including policy direction, etc.). I will never forget the pressure of that period. Some days, I remember walking into the office, down that long hallway, and muttering to myself, “This is going to be tough, this is going to be tough.” I tried to cheer myself up until I reached the door, but the truth was, it was still tough. I’ll never forget that period. But like you said, it taught me a lot and changed the way I think. To this day, I still think about liquidity, leverage, and tail risk. People always think they’ll never encounter that kind of extreme tail risk. But I think, what if that extremely unexpected situation happens and knocks me out? Of course, I’ve had to fight that fear throughout my career, telling myself, “Okay, that probably won’t happen tomorrow. We have to be in the investment business, we have to generate returns, we have to take risks.” So you have to think: Where is my escape pod? How do I think about my exit strategy for almost every asset, every position, every portfolio structure? If you know what your exit strategy is, if you know where your escape pod is, it helps you plan, and if something happens, you can execute Plan B. That being said, we’re in a risk business. I have to say, I like pressure, though not too much, but I think anyone in the investment industry must enjoy it. Host: Regarding crises, perhaps the extreme version of risk, it lets you see exactly how bad things can get. This is often attributed to Churchill's quote: "The plans of a war are useless, but the process of making those plans is essential." I feel that's your daily reality. Rieder: Absolutely. You'll find that in the investment industry, a large part of the factors driving valuations are sentiment. Markets often fall five times faster than they rise. People make money slowly, but lose it extremely quickly. Look at the recent performance of certain commodities; making money is slow, then suddenly "bang," it's all gone. You'll experience situations where a news item or event suddenly appears, and your investment logic and structural position layout are completely disrupted. Therefore, investing is not just about judging whether an asset is worthwhile, but equally important is predicting how people will interpret that asset two or three months from now. This incorporates a bit of psychology, something I learned very poorly in school, but I've picked up some of it later in my investment practice.

data.title: This AI "lobster" can trade for you—dare you give it a try?
data.description:
I came for the lobster, but stayed because of the covered call options.

If putting crustaceans and options trading together confuses you, let me clarify: I'm talking about OpenClaw, the wildly popular AI assistant that some adventurous people are currently using to manage their investments. And yes, what initially attracted me was OpenClaw's lobster-themed design.

Unlike chatbots like ChatGPT (which are practically ancient history), OpenClaw can proactively perform tasks for you, such as booking and buying show tickets. It can also provide investment strategy advice and even trade securities for you while you're lounging on the beach.

What could possibly go wrong?

What I want to say isn't that OpenClaw is some kind of marvel, but rather that AI is coming—to be precise, it's already here, and it's being used more and more by Wall Street institutions and individuals. I'm not suggesting you jump on the bandwagon, but understanding what's happening might be more beneficial. OpenClaw was created last November by Peter Steinberger (now working at OpenAI), an Austrian "ambient coding" programmer. This ecosystem, featuring lobster-themed naming conventions, reportedly boasts 38 million monthly active users, many of whom are in China. The OpenClaw channel "Friends of the Crustacean" on Discord is a popular entry point, where people frequently visit the AI. The channel is active and often highly technical (my nickname there is Andre the Seal). To better understand this, I spoke with Thomas Peterffy, Chairman of Interactive Brokers Group, who has long been at the forefront of fintech. The 81-year-old Peterffy also happens to be one of Wall Street's wealthiest people, largely due to Interactive Brokers' stock price rising from $16 to $72 since April 2022. "I was a computer programmer when I started my business, so I've always been building algorithms," Peterffy said. "For years we've been providing our clients with algorithms that automate order execution. AI is like a new, more advanced computer language. We're heavy users. There's no doubt it's the future." Interactive Brokers' clients use AI both through external AI agents like OpenClaw and through the brokerage's internal tools provided by partner company Reflexivity. “The problem has never been a lack of data, but rather that it would take days to process it. Now it only takes about eight minutes,” said Jan Siraghi, CEO and co-founder of Reflexivity. He holds a PhD in Economics and Finance from Harvard University and previously helped Stan Druckenmiller manage its global macro portfolio. On Wednesday morning, markets were jubilant after the US and Iran announced a two-week ceasefire. I asked Siraghi to set up a “TACO” trade for me (TACO being an abbreviation for “Trump always chickens out”). The algorithm returned an 11-page report concluding that “the first day of TACO trading was incredibly successful,” and recommended (along with entry points) a strong long position in crude oil and the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX); a moderate long position in the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF; a moderate short position in the S&P 500; and a slight short position in US Treasuries. Interactive Brokers rigorously vets clients who use external tools like OpenClaw. “We have procedures that capture their instructions and review them instantly to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and applicable margin rules,” Peter Fee said. What if OpenClaw sells my stocks against my will? “That’s not my problem,” he said. “That’s your problem. We just check the orders and execute them.” At the emerging stock trading platform Public.com, these kinds of questions are closer to users—the platform has gone all-in (or “fully shell-in”) on AI. Public’s clients can connect OpenClaw directly to their accounts to analyze and—yes—trade securities. Public, headquartered in New York, is a privately held company backed by Accel and Tiger Global Management venture capital. Jannick Malling, Public’s Danish co-founder and co-CEO, says the platform manages billions of dollars in assets and has millions of users. He stated that Public began exploring AI in 2023, aiming to create "the world's first AI-powered brokerage firm." The launch of OpenClaw is rapidly heating up this endeavor. "Trading has evolved from phone orders to web-based trading, then to mobile apps, and now to AI-powered brokerage," Malling said. "You just type in, 'Find me three different ways to make $10,000 this month by selling covered call options on my portfolio,' which is a huge shift." Setting up OpenClaw requires some "digital work." "It took me about an hour and a half to two hours to set it up," said Dean Allens, a 19-year-old trader from Westminster, Colorado. He may be the typical client Public is looking to attract. Allens recently used OpenClaw to build and execute a put option strategy for the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF and used it to find and buy put options on Hims & Hers Health. At the institutional level, Clive Asnes, who manages the large quantitative hedge fund AQR, was once skeptical of AI. "I might have slowed us down by two or three years," he said, "but when you have something that's worked for 30 years, it's rational to do so. All these decisions involve money, time, and risk." The 59-year-old Asnes mentioned that his fund's analysts had spent decades sifting through credit card data and analyzing the positive and negative wording in financial statements. Now, he has completely changed his mind, saying, "Machine learning—a branch of AI—has proven to be better than our past methods." Asnes said that historically, AQR's holdings were conceptually built on a 50/50 basis of historical data and economic logic. Now, thanks to AI, it's more like two-thirds data and one-third logic. Has AQR's return improved? He said, "Well, it's been a good five years." But Asnes isn't so optimistic about AI for retail investors. "I'm cautious about a world where everyone's talking about machine learning," he said. "The idea that you can use it to pick just a few stocks might not be any worse than other methods, but I don't see any magic in it." As for OpenClaw, enjoy it. Just be careful not to get caught in it.

data.title: Pharos raises $44 million in funding, with 12 projects raising $62.7 million.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This week, a total of 12 blockchain startups raised $62.7 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Pharos completed a $44 million Series A funding round, with investors including SNZ Holding, Chainlink, and Flow Traders.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed to support high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications. It is EVM compatible, allowing Ethereum dApp developers to use familiar toolchains while enjoying Pharos features such as 1-second final confirmation, reduced storage costs, and enhanced security through the AsyncBFT consensus mechanism. Pharos is committed to driving the development of DeFi, Real-World Assets (RWA), Decentralized Physical Infrastructure (DePIN), and cross-chain interoperability, and achieves innovation through a unified account system across multiple virtual machines.

**Enhanced Raises $1 Million in Pre-seed and Seed Funding Rounds** Enhanced is a DeFi protocol focused on structured options returns. Its goal is to make complex derivative strategies more accessible through an intuitive, results-oriented product experience, while improving yields by optimizing auction mechanisms and capital efficiency. The team is expanding from mainstream crypto assets to a wider range of on-chain assets, including tokenized real-world assets. **Giggles Raises $1.2 Million in Pre-seed Funding Rounds** Giggles is a Web3 social network where users can invest early in videos and earn rewards as the content spreads. Investors include 1kx, Virtuals Protocol, Social Graph Ventures, Night Capital, and Noar Ventures. By combining social interaction with market mechanisms, it creates a completely new model for content discovery and monetization. Users are not only viewers but also stakeholders, thus aligning incentives between creators and audiences. Built on blockchain, Giggles supports ownership, token rewards, and transparent interaction, creating a crypto-native ecosystem driven by viral content, memes, and community collaboration. Other Funding CAEX Exchange completed a funding round (amount undisclosed) with investors including OKX Ventures, Hashkey Capital, VPBank Securities, and LynkiD. CAEX is a crypto asset trading platform targeting the Vietnamese market, committed to providing compliant, transparent, and secure digital asset trading. Its features include compliance with Vietnamese laws and regulations, KYC/KYB verification, trading based on the Vietnamese Dong (VND), customer asset segregation, and multi-layered security mechanisms including cold wallets and HSMs. The platform positions itself as a trusted gateway for investors seeking a locally compliant trading experience. Oh has completed a $7.5 million Series A funding round, with investors including Maven 11 Capital, L1D, Auros Global, Hashed, Maelstrom, Cadenza, Cyber ​​Fund, Kosmos Ventures, and Tangent Ventures. Oh is a Web3 AI project dedicated to developing uncensored generative AI models and applications. Its ecosystem includes OhChat (a platform for immersive interaction with AI characters "superModels") and the $OH token (used for platform governance and functionality). Oh aims to decentralize AI governance and provide monetization opportunities for creators through interoperable AI characters. Transak has completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed), with Gobi Partners as the investor. Transak is a Web3 payment infrastructure provider offering seamless exchange (deposits and withdrawals) between fiat and cryptocurrency. Designed for developers, it can be easily integrated into wallets, dApps, and platforms via SDK or API, and supports multiple payment methods (bank cards, bank transfers, Apple Pay, Google Pay), covering 155+ countries, and includes a KYC process. Transak also offers advanced tools such as Transak One (one-step fiat to smart contract) and Transak Stream (fast withdrawal to bank accounts), and holds compliance licenses in the US, Canada, the UK, and other countries. Libera has completed its Series A funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including GSR Markets LTD, Monk’s Hill Ventures, and AlloyX. Libera is a blockchain-based platform focused on the tokenization of Real-World Assets (RWAs), enabling institutions to access traditionally illiquid financial products. Its goal is to connect traditional finance with Web3, providing the infrastructure for issuing, managing, and distributing tokenized funds, emphasizing compliance and transparency. Splyce Finance has completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including the Sui Foundation, Stellar Development Foundation, Solana Foundation, Lucid Drakes, Sarson Funds, and Kin Capital. Splyce Finance is a Solana-based DeFi protocol focused on delivering real-world yields through fully on-chain institutional-grade assets. It supports permissionless access to tokenized RWAs and automated yield strategies while maintaining composability and 24/7 liquidity. The platform also offers decentralized ETFs (dETFs), allowing users to diversify their crypto asset portfolios through a single token. Darklake has completed an acquisition funding round (amount undisclosed), with Sol Strategies (formerly Cypherpunk Holdings) as the investor. Darklake is a Solana-based zero-knowledge infrastructure project dedicated to building privacy-first systems for on-chain applications. Its technology stack supports secure computation and collaboration for applications, wallets, and protocols while maintaining composability and Solana's high-speed settlement. Its products include a privacy-preserving ZK-AMM, a perpetual DEX under development, and the core proof technology Zyga Protocol. The project focuses on reusable proofs, cryptographic states, and developer-oriented tools. NanoVita has completed its Series A funding round (amount undisclosed), with investors including K24 Ventures, Landscape Capital, and WestLabs Venture. NanoVita is a crypto-native DeSci protocol that integrates nanotechnology, AI bio-intelligence, and tokenized health data into a unified on-chain ecosystem. It enables users to own and monetize their health data, while supporting decentralized research and personalized medicine. By combining AI inference, blockchain transparency, and the RWA dataset, NanoVita transforms research findings into verifiable on-chain value. GoSats has raised $5 million in Series A funding, with investors including Konvoy Ventures, Y Combinator, and Taisu Ventures. GoSats is a Bitcoin rewards platform where users can earn "sats" (the smallest unit of Bitcoin) by shopping at partner brands. It combines everyday spending with crypto applications, offering Bitcoin cashback instead of traditional fiat currency rewards. The platform also offers gift cards, brand partnerships, and gamified rewards to increase user engagement and long-term accumulation of crypto assets. INXY Payments has raised $4 million in funding, with Flashpoint Venture Capital as the investor. INXY Payments is an EU-licensed fintech platform providing multi-currency payment processing solutions for online businesses. Its services include funds gateways, large-scale payments, and currency exchange, supporting one-off and recurring transfers, automated payroll, and asset conversion. The platform emphasizes flexible integration via API, low-cost transactions, and compliance with mainstream auditing standards. It also provides reporting features suitable for accounting use, including complete transaction records and customized reports.

data.title: Binance launches prediction market; Hong Kong issues first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses.
data.description:
Golden Weekly is a weekly blockchain industry summary column launched by Golden Finance, covering key news, mining information, project updates, technological advancements, and other industry dynamics. This article is the news segment of the weekly summary, giving you a glimpse into the major events in the blockchain industry this week.

Headlines

▌Circle has minted a total of 10.5 billion USDC on Solana in the past month

On April 11th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, Circle minted an additional 250 million USDC. In the past month, Circle has minted a total of 10.5 billion USDC on Solana.

On April 11, Du Guangda, Deputy Director of the Science and Technology Department of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, stated that the Ministry will focus on manufacturing as the main battlefield and application-driven development as the main line, releasing a batch of high-value "AI+" scenarios, exploring a batch of typical applications, building a batch of distinctive intelligent entities, providing a batch of new intelligent terminals, developing a batch of new standards, cultivating a batch of industrial application talents, and creating a batch of high-quality enterprises, comprehensively promoting the deep integration of artificial intelligence and manufacturing. (Xinhua) On April 12, according to Iran's Fars News Agency, an Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson said that Iran and the United States reached consensus on some issues, but disagreed on two or three important issues, ultimately failing to reach an agreement. (Xinhua) On April 8, TikTok announced it would invest 1 billion euros to build a second data center in Lahti, Finland, marking the company's second 1 billion euro data center investment project in Finland. The statement said the investment is part of TikTok's €12 billion "Project Clover," which aims to process and protect the data of over 200 million European users. TikTok also stated that it is progressing as planned with the Kouvola data center project in Finland, announced last year. (Jiemian News) On April 12, US President Trump retweeted an article on his social media, stating that if Iran refuses to compromise, its trump card might be controlling the Strait of Hormuz through the US Navy to prevent Iranian oil tankers from entering or leaving, thereby striking at the Iranian economy. (CCTV News) The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) announced on April 10 that the Commissioner of Finance has granted stablecoin issuer licenses to Dianxin Financial Technology Co., Ltd. (a joint venture between Standard Chartered Bank, Animoca Brands, and Hong Kong Telecom) and HSBC Bank Limited under the Stablecoin Ordinance, allowing them to issue stablecoins in Hong Kong. The licenses took effect today. According to the licensees' business plans, they intend to commence operations in the coming months after completing the necessary preparatory work.

▌“Guideline for Risk Management of OpenClaw-like Intelligent Agent Deployment” Officially Released

On April 10th, during the 17th Plenary Session of the China Artificial Intelligence Industry Development Alliance, the Security Governance Committee of the China Artificial Intelligence Industry Development Alliance officially released the “Guideline for Risk Management of OpenClaw-like Intelligent Agent Deployment.” To systematically address the security challenges posed by OpenClaw-like intelligent agents, the guideline focuses on the pain points of risk prevention and control for enterprises during the technology implementation process. It proposes an overall framework and self-inspection standards for secure deployment covering the entire process of deployment, use, and decommissioning. The aim is to promote the advancement of intelligent agents from “deployable” to “secure deployment, standardized use, and continuous governance,” providing practical guidance for enterprises to conduct standardized technology deployment.

▌Comptroller of the Currency: The U.S. Should Not Hand Over Digital Asset Innovation to Other Countries Due to Regulatory Uncertainty

On April 12, the Comptroller of the Currency stated that the U.S. should not hand over digital asset innovation to other countries due to regulatory uncertainty. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessenter emphasized that clarity is key to maintaining domestic markets, businesses, capital, and technology. A clear framework is how we ensure the U.S. maintains its dominance and advantage in financial innovation, in line with Trump's vision of U.S. leadership in the global economy.

Blockchain Applications

▌Animoca Brands will launch the Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin HKDAP in phases starting in Q2 this year

Animoca Brands announced on the X platform that Animoca Brands, a joint venture established by Standard Chartered Bank, HKT and Animoca Brands, will launch the regulated Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin HKDAP (HKD At Par) in phases starting in the second quarter of this year. Group President Evan Auyang stated that a properly governed Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin is crucial to Hong Kong's financial infrastructure.

Animoca Brands announced on the X platform that Animoca Brands, a joint venture established by Standard Chartered Bank, HKT and Animoca Brands, will launch the regulated Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin HKDAP (HKD At Par) in phases starting in the second quarter of this year.

Animoca Brands Group President Evan Auyang stated that a properly governed Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin is crucial to Hong Kong's financial infrastructure.

▌Animoca Brands will launch the Hong Kong dollar-backed stablecoin HKDAP (HKD At Par) in phases starting in Q2 this year ...strong>

▌Meta Pledges Additional $21 Billion Investment in CoreWeave

On April 9th, according to CNBC: Meta Platforms (META.O) pledged an additional $21 billion investment in CoreWeave.

▌Binance Officially Launches Prediction Market

On April 9th, Binance announced the launch of its prediction market. This new feature allows users to participate in probability-based markets from the Binance app through integration with third-party platforms. Upon launch, the Binance Wallet will integrate market access provided by Predict.fun, a leading on-chain prediction market provider on the BNB Smart Chain (BSC). In the Binance App, go to the [Markets] tab. Click the [Prediction] tab (regions only).

Select a category to browse available marketplaces that offer real-time probability pricing.

    ▌Coinbase Obtains Australian Financial Services License, Plans to Expand Stock Trading and Payments Business

    On April 8th, Coinbase announced that it will expand its business to stock trading and payments in Australia after obtaining a financial services license. The company stated that it has obtained an Australian Financial Services License (AFSL) from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC), covering retail derivatives authorization, and stated that it is the first crypto exchange to receive such approval directly from the regulator.

      ▌Polygon Foundation: Giugliano Hard Fork to Take Place on Polygon Mainnet on April 8th UTC

      On April 7th, the Polygon Foundation announced that the Giugliano hard fork will be launched on the Polygon mainnet at block height 85,268,500, expected to take place at 2 PM UTC on April 8th. This upgrade includes: faster finality by allowing block producers to broadcast blocks earlier; adding fee parameters directly to the block header; and adding RPC support for fee data.

      All node operators must upgrade Bor to v2.7.0 (or Erigon to v3.5.0).

        Cryptocurrency

        ▌SpaceX still holds $603 million worth of Bitcoin despite a nearly $5 billion loss in 2025

        On April 12th, according to Arkham data and a report released Friday evening by The Information, SpaceX currently holds 8,285 Bitcoins in its Coinbase Prime escrow account, worth approximately $603 million, while the company is projected to lose nearly $5 billion in 2025. This loss is significantly different from the previous year, when SpaceX achieved a profit of approximately $8 billion in 2024, with revenue estimated between $15 billion and $16 billion.

        While SpaceX's revenue is projected to reach $18.5 billion in 2025, the integration costs of xAI, Musk's AI company, into SpaceX in February of this year outweighed the revenue growth. Despite significant losses, SpaceX's Bitcoin holdings have remained unchanged. CoinDesk's analysis of transaction records shows that the most recent major fund movement occurred approximately four months ago, a rebalancing operation involving the transfer of 614 and 1,021 Bitcoins between SpaceX's internal wallets. The holdings have remained stable since mid-2024, having previously exceeded $1.6 billion in market value during Bitcoin's all-time high in October 2025. SpaceX is currently the fourth-largest known company in the world in terms of Bitcoin holdings, after Strategy, Marathon Digital, and Riot Platforms. SpaceX has filed for an IPO, at which time its Bitcoin holdings will appear in public filings for the first time and may be required to undergo fair value accounting under the new FASB guidelines that will take effect at the end of 2025.

        ▌Sources: Gemini Plans to Convert Winklevoss Twins' Approximately $330 Million Loan into Equity

        On April 11, according to Bloomberg, Gemini (GEMI), the cryptocurrency exchange founded by the Winklevoss twins, has seen its market capitalization more than halved this year, with its stock price falling by over 50% cumulatively. The company has already laid off approximately 30% of its staff and withdrawn from major overseas markets such as the UK, EU, and Australia, facing increasing financial pressure. Gemini is internally discussing a plan: requesting the founding twins to forgive or convert approximately $330 million in loans they provided into equity to alleviate its debt burden. These loans were primarily provided through Winklevoss Capital Fund LLC. As of the end of December 2025, Gemini still owed 4,619 bitcoins (worth over $330 million at the time). If this move is realized, as the twins are the main controllers of the company, it will directly impact the company's capital structure.

        Following the report's release, Gemini's stock price initially rose by approximately 9%, but still fell significantly for the year. The company has previously experienced executive departures, a shift in business towards prediction markets, and shareholder lawsuits; this loan focus further highlights the ongoing impact of the crypto winter on exchanges.

        ▌TD Cowen Analyst Lowers MSTR Stock Price Target from $440 to $350

        On April 11th, Lance Vitanza, an analyst at the $15 billion investment bank TD Cowen, lowered the price target for Bitcoin treasury company Strategy ($MSTR) from $440 to $350, but maintained a "buy" rating.

        ...

        ▌Grayscale Releases Latest List of Candidate Assets, Adding HYPE, STX, VIRTUAL, etc.

        According to official news, digital asset investment platform Grayscale recently updated its list of candidate assets for its products, covering six major crypto sectors. The newly added candidate assets are particularly concentrated in the AI ​​and finance categories. The AI ​​sector has the most newly added candidate assets, including Fabric Protocol, Flock, Grass, Kaito, Kite AI, Nous Research, Poseidon, Venice, and Virtuals Protocol, totaling 9 projects. These are considered alongside Bittensor, Livepeer, Near, Render, and Story, reflecting the continued focus of institutions on the AI ​​+ crypto sector.

        In the financial sector, seven DeFi projects—Ethena, Hyperliquid, Jupiter, Kamino Finance, Maple Finance, Morpho, and Pendle—have been added to the shortlist, complementing existing offerings such as Aave, Aerodrome, Curve, Ondo, and Uniswap. The smart contract platform sector has added six projects: Celo, Mantle, MegaETH, Monad, Toncoin, and Tron, with Canton Network also included. The utility tools sector has added DoubleZero, Geodnet, Helium, Jito, LayerZero, and Wormhole. Currently, Grayscale's existing product suite covers major assets including BTC, ETH, SOL, XRP, AVAX, BNB, and ADA. The shortlist does not guarantee the launch of related products; Grayscale states that it will update the list within 15 days after the end of each quarter.

        ▌Galaxy Research Director: Whether Iran Uses Bitcoin to Collect Strait of Hormuz Passage Fees Remains Controversial

        On April 11, Alex Thorn, Research Director of Galaxy, published a lengthy analysis on the X platform, stating that reports regarding Iran demanding ships pay Strait of Hormuz passage fees in Bitcoin have not been fully verified. Previously, the Financial Times reported that Iran planned to charge $1 per barrel of oil and require payment in Bitcoin; however, information from various sources differs. Bloomberg reported that the fees might be settled in stablecoins or the yuan, while research firm Citrini Research pointed out that some ships completed payments by unfreezing Iranian overseas assets. Blockchain analytics firm TRM Labs also stated that there is currently no evidence that cryptocurrencies are being used on a large scale for such passage fee payments.

        Alex Thorn believes that if it's true that Bitcoin is used to collect tolls, Bitcoin has certain advantages as a censorship-resistant payment tool. However, the claim that "fast payments enhance privacy" is inaccurate unless layer-two solutions like the Lightning Network are used. Furthermore, considering that a single toll could range from $200,000 to $2 million, large on-chain or Lightning Network payments still face practical limitations. Alex Thorn revealed that Galaxy Research will conduct cross-analysis of ship AIS data and on-chain transactions to determine if there are corresponding-scale Bitcoin transactions. While there are historical precedents of Iran using crypto assets for cross-border transactions, the claim of "Bitcoin collecting tolls" still needs further verification.

        Important Economic Developments

        ▌Polish Central Bank to Continue Gold Purchases

        April 9th ​​news: Polish Central Bank Governor Grapinski stated that the Polish Central Bank will continue to purchase gold.

        Poland reiterated its goal of increasing its gold reserves to 700 tons; the central bank currently holds 580 tons. White House National Economic Council Director Hassett: He expects Powell not to remain on the Federal Reserve Board. He is confident that Warsh will become Federal Reserve Chairman in May. Bloomberg Strategist: Gold's Parabolic Peak May Indicate Risk of US Stock Market Correction Bloomberg Intelligence Senior Commodity Strategist Mike McGlone wrote on the X platform that historical experience shows that when gold prices peak after a rapid rise, US stocks often follow suit. Currently, gold prices have risen to approximately 1.9 times their 20-quarter moving average, higher than the peak of approximately 1.7 times in 2008. If gold reverts to its long-term average, the S&P 500 index may face a correction of approximately 25%; a similar situation in 2008 triggered a drop of approximately 60%. Driven by factors such as the global energy crisis, both gold and stocks are at high levels. Even a mean reversion at this stage could exert downward pressure on the market.

        ▌US March CPI rose 3.3% year-on-year, a new high since May 2024

data.title: The TACO ceasefire merely put the Iran war on hold.
data.description:
Authors: Catherine Lucey, Courtney Subramanian, Eric Martin, and Jennifer A. Dlouhy, Bloomberg; Translator: Peggy, BlockBeats

From a "Stone Age" threat to a swift two-week ceasefire, the conflict surrounding Iran has undergone a dramatic shift in a short period. On the surface, the situation has cooled and markets have rebounded, but deeper structural problems have not been resolved.

On the one hand, Donald Trump, under political and economic pressure, chose to "step down," temporarily alleviating market and ally anxiety through a ceasefire; on the other hand, Iran has strengthened its control over the Strait of Hormuz during the conflict and is attempting to transform this geopolitical advantage into long-term leverage. The so-called "ceasefire" is more like a time-for-space arrangement than a true end to the problem.

Meanwhile, divisions within the United States are also intensifying. Significant cracks have emerged within the Republican Party regarding whether to cross the line into war and whether to accept an agreement that falls far short of its core objectives.

From the market's dramatic fluctuations to the constant shifts in policy statements, this crisis has not only exposed the highly personalized nature of decision-making mechanisms but has also prompted the world to reassess America's credibility and execution capabilities. With no clear victor, the conflict is entering a more complex phase: military deterrence, energy maneuvering, and political negotiations are intertwined. The real question is no longer "whether to cease fire," but how this game will be repriced, given that all parties' bottom lines remain firm. The following is the original text: US President Donald Trump has two weeks to determine whether he has untied the knot he himself tied on the Iran issue, or has instead tightened it further. His announcement on Tuesday of accepting a two-week ceasefire proposal with Iran brought relief to major capital cities around the world and jubilation to financial markets. At least for now, concerns have been temporarily set aside: that he will continue his attacks on civilian infrastructure and drag the region into deeper conflict through a bombing campaign he once described as "blowing Iran back to the Stone Age." But amidst this celebratory atmosphere of yet another Taco Tuesday, the president, known for his last-minute retreats, has once again teetered on the brink of disaster. A more realistic realization has emerged: none of the core issues have actually been resolved. Note: "Taco Tuesday" is a cultural phenomenon. In the United States, many restaurants offer discounts on tacos on Tuesdays, and over time, "Taco Tuesday" has become a lighthearted, celebratory, and even somewhat entertaining expression. One crucial unresolved issue is whether the Strait of Hormuz has truly restored passage for oil tankers. Despite some vague signals from Iran suggesting it would allow more ships to pass through the waterway, the reality remains unclear. Iran has shown little willingness to accept all of the US demands, whether it's weakening the existing regime or installing a pro-US leadership like Venezuela. Meanwhile, Tehran has not publicly committed to meeting Trump's two core demands: permanently abandoning its nuclear program and completely decommissioning its ballistic missile system. Moreover, the US president has previously threatened to "eradicate Iranian civilization," a statement that, if carried out, could cross the line into war crimes. On April 6, US President Donald Trump held a press conference at the White House. (Photo: Aaron Schwartz / CNP / Bloomberg)

Meanwhile, Trump confirmed that a "ten-point plan" proposed by Iran will serve as the basis for future negotiations. Tehran has previously demanded the lifting of sanctions and compensation for losses caused by the war. This could also mean imposing new fees on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, thus keeping shipping costs and energy prices high for an extended period.

From a practical perspective, for a US president under significant political and economic pressure and urgently needing to find a "way downhill," even if these goals cannot be fully achieved, he may have to accept it.

"It's not surprising that Trump clearly wants to postpone the issue again," said Ian Bremmer, founder of the Eurasia Group.

According to a senior government official who declined to be named, the White House deliberately created an atmosphere of suspense as Tuesday's 8 p.m. deadline approached. This was partly because the president himself instructed his advisors not to release any signals, leaving the outside world to speculate on his next move. At the same time, the US intensified its strikes against Iranian military targets, including its main oil export hub, Kharg Island, to increase its leverage in negotiations and demonstrate its military capabilities. According to a White House official, Vice President JD Vance played a central role in Tuesday's decision-making process, despite being in Hungary meeting with Viktor Orbán. At one point, Vance took Donald Trump on speakerphone at a rally in Orbán; the official also stated that the vice president had privately communicated directly with a Pakistani official acting as a mediator. The official added that Trump continued consultations with senior U.S. national security officials and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu throughout the day, and ultimately spoke with a Pakistani field marshal to confirm a ceasefire agreement. Meanwhile, the West Wing of the White House was abuzz with discussion over the release of a report that revealed widespread skepticism within the Trump administration prior to the outbreak of war regarding Israel's assessment that a military strike could incite a popular uprising in Iran and bring about a new secular government. The report also revealed Vance's initial opposition to the strike, as well as concerns expressed privately by several other senior aides. Against this backdrop, allies within Trump's political coalition spent much of Tuesday warning him against fulfilling his earlier extreme threat to "end Persian civilization." Conservative podcast hosts even openly discussed whether the cabinet should consider removing Donald Trump from office; even Republicans on Capitol Hill who have consistently supported him said his threats to crack down on power plants and desalination facilities had crossed the line. Although Trump's Republican Party won a special election in Georgia that day, the margin in this traditionally considered a "safe seat" narrowed significantly, seen as a potential sign of voter discontent. Meanwhile, economic pressures are mounting. Since the conflict erupted in late February, West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil prices have risen nearly 70%, pushing gas prices above $4 per gallon for the first time in years. Within an hour of Trump's announcement, oil prices fell 11%, highlighting the market's pent-up anxiety about his policy direction. The S&P 500 Index has fallen about 5.2% from its all-time high and just recorded its worst quarterly performance since 2022. Oil prices plummeted after the US and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire. Brent crude fell as much as 16% on Wednesday. Trump's decision was made at the last minute. According to a US official, the president only fully heard Pakistan's proposal late Tuesday afternoon, about two hours after he announced the ceasefire on Truth Social. Whether to accept the proposal, and whether it met his own conditions, ultimately depended entirely on Donald Trump himself. Unknown Details Despite the president's claim in his statement that he had "moved this long-standing problem to near resolution," publicly available information offers little indication that this military and economic predicament, which has severely impacted his political standing, is moving towards genuine easing. Jennifer Kavanagh, director of military analysis at the libertarian think tank Defense Priorities, wrote on social media: "Trump's decision to back down tonight is certainly a relief. But if he was already prepared to back down, it was arguably the worst possible way. His previous escalation of the stakes has ultimately damaged his credibility and the perception of American power. This is a clear strategic failure." Within hours of Trump's ceasefire announcement, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt urged caution regarding reports of face-to-face talks, stating that any substantial progress must be confirmed by the president's official announcement. Meanwhile, according to Al Jazeera, Iran's proposals include demanding the withdrawal of US troops from their bases and deployments in the region and the unfreezing of Iranian assets. The likelihood of either the US or Israel accepting these conditions seems extremely slim. Although Washington has largely dismissed Iran's public statements throughout the conflict, believing Tehran is more flexible in private negotiations, this stark contrast between public and private statements highlights the deep-seated divisions and distrust that Trump must currently address. Jonathan Panikoff points out that a long-term solution requires addressing a series of core issues: the future of Iran's nuclear and missile programs, the fate of its highly enriched uranium stockpile, and the permanent guarantee of freedom of navigation. He stated, "With Iran willing to open the Strait of Hormuz, the president's acceptance of a two-week ceasefire certainly provided him with a 'stepping stone,' allowing him to declare a partial victory." Shortly after the ceasefire announcement, Trump, in an interview with AFP, stated that Iran's uranium stockpile would be "properly handled," without specifying the method, while still referring to the agreement as a "complete victory" for the United States. Energy and the "Energy Off-Ramp" Throughout his public career, Trump has consistently been less committed to aligning predetermined goals with reality; he has repeatedly paved the way for his "retreat" while key issues remain unresolved. He has also retreated from the most radical policies and threats on numerous occasions. For example, a year ago, after a sharp decline in financial markets, he quickly made concessions just days after implementing global tariffs. This administration may be pinning its hopes on: voters' short memories, the gradual recovery of the economy after the conflict's shock, and Iran's retaliation—something even Trump himself admitted was unexpected—which time can heal. However, if a deal close to the president's initial vision fails to materialize, the White House will inevitably face serious scrutiny. Jon Hoffman, a defense and foreign policy researcher at the Cato Institute, stated, "Overall, Trump's initial goals were extremely aggressive, including destroying Iran's nuclear program and even pushing for regime change, but neither of those has been achieved." Connecticut Democratic Senator Chris Murphy warned that if an agreement ultimately solidifies Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz, it will have "catastrophic consequences" for the world. In an interview with CNN, he stated, "It doesn't even sound like a real agreement right now because Trump's statements are completely inconsistent with Iran's. What a serious miscalculation it would be if Iran permanently controls the Strait, and how much of a failure this whole operation would appear." Trump's inconsistent rhetoric. Trump's public statements on the Iran war have repeatedly triggered sharp fluctuations in oil prices. For the United States, a rather ironic reality is that the five-week-long war may actually give Tehran more leverage in the global economy. During the conflict, Iran effectively strengthened its control over the Strait of Hormuz, virtually closing it to countries it considers "hostile," and is now pushing forward a plan to impose "passage fees" on oil tankers transiting the waterway. Clayton Seigle, a senior fellow at the Center for Strategic and International Studies in Washington, stated, "It is highly unlikely that Iran will relinquish its newly acquired, or at least strengthened, claim to control of the strait." Kevin Book, managing director of ClearView Energy Partners, headquartered in Washington, pointed out that the market views this ceasefire as a significant signal largely because it includes a commitment to reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

A naval vessel sails in the Strait of Hormuz on March 1, 2026. (Photo: Sahar Al Attar / AFP / Getty Images)

Meanwhile, the possibility of a significant drop in oil prices in the short term remains low.

Kevin Book, Managing Director of ClearView Energy Partners, said, "The lower end of the oil price range is still roughly around $100 per barrel." He added that if Donald Trump had actually carried out the threatened strike and triggered Iranian retaliation, the world could have faced an "unlimited" surge in oil prices.

GOP Offsides

Trump also needs to repair the divisions that have surfaced within his party on this issue. On Tuesday, a clear rift emerged within the Republican Party over whether to take a tougher stance against Iran.

Before the ceasefire, Trump warned that "tonight a whole civilization will be destroyed." In response, Texas Representative Nathaniel Moran, a Trump endorser, expressed concern on social media, saying that the United States is deviating from "the long-standing principles guiding America's national defense."

Wisconsin Republican Senator Ron Johnson stated that launching an attack on civilian infrastructure would be "a huge mistake." Wisconsin Senator Ron Johnson. (Photo: Brendan Smialowski / AFP / Getty Images) "If he attacks civilian targets, I can no longer support him. Whatever action we take must comply with the laws of war," Johnson told The Wall Street Journal. He also told podcast host John Solomon on Monday, "I hope, and I pray, that Trump is just bluffing. We are not at odds with the Iranian people; our goal is to 'liberate' them." Several former Trump allies, including Tucker Carlson, former Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene, and former White House official Anthony Scaramucci, have joined calls for his resignation, precisely because of his previous threats. After Trump shifted to a de-escalation stance, conservative opinion leaders Laura Loomer and Senator Lindsey Graham, who were previously key supporters of his hardline stance against Iran, began expressing concerns about the ceasefire agreement.

South Carolina Senator Lindsey Graham. (Photo: Anna Moneymaker / Getty Images)

“I don’t understand why anyone would treat this as a victory,” conservative opinion leader Laura Loomer wrote on social media. “Muslim communities, the so-called ‘Woke Reich,’ and the anti-Trump camp are using this ‘negotiation’ to attack President Trump while praising Tucker Carlson’s allies and calling for the invocation of the 25th Amendment against Trump.”

The Republican senator from South Carolina stated that Americans “must remember that after the outbreak of war, it was Iran that attacked the Strait of Hormuz, undermining freedom of navigation.”

The Republican senator from South Carolina stated that Americans “must remember that after the outbreak of war, it was Iran that attacked the Strait of Hormuz, violating freedom of navigation.”

"The crucial next step is to ensure that Iran does not gain anything from this hostile act against the world," Graham wrote on social media. He added that the United States must ensure control over "every bit" of highly enriched uranium within Iran. "Time will tell," the senator said.
